title: Dog Squinting Left Eye Tightly Shut: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog squinting one eye? Discover common causes, the urgency level for vet care, and how a photo helps with professional triage.
content_html:
What Does Squinting Mean?

When a dog squints or keeps an eye tightly shut, it is medically known as blepharospasm. This is a primary sign of ocular pain. Because the cornea is packed with sensitive nerve endings, even a minor irritation can cause a dog to clamp the eyelid shut to protect the globe. Common causes range from a simple foreign object like a grass seed to more serious conditions like corneal ulcers, uveitis, or glaucoma.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While squinting is not usually an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is considered a 'medical priority.' Eye conditions can deteriorate very quickly, sometimes within hours. You should aim to have your dog seen by a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours. If the eye appears cloudy, bleeding, or if the dog is pawing at it incessantly, seek urgent care immediately to prevent permanent vision loss.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality photo to a veterinary professional can significantly speed up the triage process. A clear image allows the vet to look for signs of swelling, discharge, or visible foreign bodies without needing to immediately touch the painful area. It also helps them determine if the issue is on the surface of the eye or deep within the ocular structure, allowing them to prioritize your appointment appropriately.

faqs.question: Should I try to wash the eye out at home?
faqs.answer: Unless you see a specific piece of debris that can be easily flushed with plain saline, it is best to avoid touching the eye. Incorrectly flushing a scratched cornea can cause further pain or damage.

faqs.question: Can I use my own human eye drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No. Never use human medications or leftover veterinary drops from a previous issue. Some drops contain steroids which can cause a corneal ulcer to worsen rapidly, potentially leading to a ruptured eye.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from rubbing the eye?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent self-trauma is to use an Elizabethan collar (the cone). This prevents the dog from scratching the eye with their paws or rubbing it against furniture.
